## 2026-05-16 - codex - HELP01 contextual route guidance

**Goal:** Add lightweight route guidance so users can understand each main
product route without owner narration or modal-tour dependency.

**Did:**

- Reviewed the HELP01 plan, current self-serve UX contract, frontend product
  surface architecture, current handoff queue, and relevant selected-series
  route code.
- Performed a timeboxed v1 reference review of shared guide templates,
  employee guide partials, layout help hooks, respondent landing, and individual
  statistics views.
- Added `apps/web/src/lib/product/route-guidance.ts` and
  `route-guidance.test.ts` as the typed route guidance model.
- Added shared `apps/web/src/lib/components/RouteGuidancePanel.svelte`.
- Rendered route-owned guidance on `/app`, `/app/campaign-series`,
  selected-study overview, Prepare, Collect, Review results, Waves,
  `/app/exports`, `/app/instruments`, `/app/directory`, `/app/team`, and
  `/app/settings`.
- Tightened generic sample/own/study empty states so they point to visible
  sample or own work instead of campaign-series technical wording.
- Updated `NEXT-ACTIONS.md`, `NEXT-SESSION-PLAN.md`, backlog, current roadmap,
  self-serve UX docs, and frontend product-surface docs so HELP01 is done and
  `D125` is the current queue head.

**V1 reference review:** v1 leaned on modal guide templates, instruction
buttons, and tooltips to explain complex screens. HELP01 ported the useful
lesson as persistent inline route-owned guidance, not a hidden modal tour or
help-center dependency.

**Decisions:** Route guidance remains frontend-only over existing route and
view-model state. It should explain purpose, first inspection target,
role/sample limits, and next move. It must not add modal tours, coach marks, a
help center, a new dependency, duplicate-to-edit, backend APIs, migrations,
seed refresh, broad visual redesign, real-person data approval, legal/GDPR
claims, deployment automation, or platform-canonical named-instrument content.

**Open questions raised:** None. Q-053 remains a real-person/legal-use gate
only and does not block synthetic/demo/sample/owner-controlled product work.

## 2026-05-16 - codex - DUP01 duplicate sample study into editable own study

**Goal:** Add the promised sample-to-own transition so a setup manager can turn
a read-only sample study into editable tenant-owned work.

**Did:**

- Added `POST /campaign-series/{id}/duplicate` with
  `DuplicateCampaignSeriesRequest` and `CampaignSeriesDuplicateResponse`.
- Implemented `DuplicateCampaignSeriesCommand` and
  `ProductSurfaceWriteStore.DuplicateCampaignSeriesAsync`.
- Copied only the editable setup skeleton: campaign-series shell, fresh salt,
  usable consent/retention/disclosure policies, and draft campaign shells with
  existing template versions.
- Kept operational evidence out of the copy: launch snapshots, assignments,
  invitation tokens, notifications, response sessions, answers, score runs,
  scores, report/export artifacts, archived state, and sample labels.
- Added frontend API/view-model support plus `Duplicate as study` buttons on
  sample rows in `/app/campaign-series` and in the selected sample read-only
  state on `/app/campaign-series/{seriesId}`.
- Updated `NEXT-ACTIONS.md`, `NEXT-SESSION-PLAN.md`, backlog, current roadmap,
  self-serve UX docs, and frontend product-surface docs so `D126` is the
  current queue head.

**V1 reference review:** Timeboxed read-only review of v1 survey-structure
clone flows found module/submodule clone commands that regenerate identities,
copy configuration graph rows transactionally, show busy/success UI, and select
the new clone. DUP01 ported the lesson as a transactional setup-skeleton copy
and route-to-copy behavior, not v1 code.

**Decisions:** Duplicate is setup-authorized and only valid for sample studies.
The copy is a normal own study. Campaign draft `createdBy` is left null and the
transaction audit context records the actor, because integration coverage
proved seeded fixture actors are not guaranteed to satisfy the campaign
created-by FK.

**Open questions raised:** None. Q-053 remains a real-person/legal-use gate
only and does not block synthetic/demo/sample/owner-controlled product work.

## 2026-05-16 - codex - SEEDUX02 starter sample state refresh

**Goal:** Make starter validation samples teach the first-session product path
after DUP01: setup readiness, collection in progress, results-ready work, and
longitudinal waves.

**Did:**

- Updated `deploy/staging/validation-demo-fixtures/validation-demo-catalog.json`
  so each validation tenant story declares setup and in-collection starter
  series in addition to the existing mixed-lifecycle and longitudinal series.
- Updated `deploy/staging/seed-validation-demo.ps1` so catalog validation
  requires setup, in-collection, completed/results, and longitudinal sample
  coverage.
- Updated live seed behavior so each tenant creates four normal
  `study_kind=sample` campaign series through product APIs and then asserts
  home plus Studies expose the four required read-only sample states.
- Updated frontend sample ownership copy so sample read-only messages name the
  scenario as `Setup sample`, `Collection sample`, `Results sample`, or
  `Longitudinal sample` instead of generic starter-content copy.
- Updated product view-model and Playwright coverage for the new user-facing
  sample-state messages.
- Updated validation fixture README, `NEXT-ACTIONS.md`, `NEXT-SESSION-PLAN.md`,
  backlog, current roadmap, self-serve UX docs, and frontend product-surface
  docs so `D127` is the current queue head.

**TDD / red checks:**

- `deploy/staging/seed-validation-demo.ps1 -ValidateOnly` first failed with
  `Tenant validation-oh-research story.setupSeriesName must not be blank.`
  after adding the required starter-state coverage validation.
- `node node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run src/lib/product/view-models.test.ts
  -t "maps sample scenarios"` first failed because sample messages were still
  the generic `Sample studies are read-only starter content.` copy.

**Decisions:** SEEDUX02 keeps starter samples as normal tenant-private
proof/demo data, not `/app/demo` fixtures. The setup sample has no campaigns so
it appears as setup/blocked. The collection sample has a live campaign with a
saved but unsubmitted response so it remains in-collection. The existing
mixed-lifecycle series remains the results-ready sample because it carries
submitted responses, scores, report proof, and exports. The longitudinal series
remains the waves sample.

**Open questions raised:** None. Q-053 remains a real-person/legal-use gate
only and does not block synthetic/demo/sample/owner-controlled product work.

## 2026-05-16 - codex - QA03 self-serve walkthrough contract

**Goal:** Add an executable contract and owner-run validation support for the
first-session self-serve path selected by D127.

**Did:**

- Added a focused Playwright contract in
  `apps/web/src/routes/product-surfaces.e2e.ts` covering the normal route path:
  home lifecycle, four starter sample states, Studies sample rows, selected
  sample overview, selected setup inspection, read-only analyst/viewer sample
  behavior, setup-manager `Duplicate as study`, and routing the duplicated own
  study to setup.
- Expanded the frontend home-route test fixture so sample studies include setup
  readiness, collection in progress, results-ready, and longitudinal sample
  states.
- Extended `deploy/staging/smoke-validation-demo-preflight.ps1` with a
  non-destructive self-serve walkthrough contract check over the selected
  validation tenant's committed catalog story metadata.
- Updated deployment package coverage, validation fixture README, and the
  validation walkthrough packet so owner-run preflight output names the
  self-serve route checklist.
- Updated `NEXT-ACTIONS.md`, `NEXT-SESSION-PLAN.md`, backlog, current roadmap,
  self-serve UX docs, and frontend product-surface docs so `D128` is the
  current queue head.

**TDD / red checks:**

- Direct Playwright with the configured webServer failed before assertions
  because this shell still cannot resolve `npm` for the configured webServer.
  The explicit Vite preview path was used instead.
- The new Playwright contract first failed because the home sample fixture did
  not expose `Setup readiness sample`.
- The deployment-package test first failed because
  `smoke-validation-demo-preflight.ps1` did not reference
  `validation-demo-catalog.json` or the self-serve walkthrough contract.
- Full product-surface Playwright first failed on an old strict locator that
  expected one `Sample study` badge; the assertion now expects four home sample
  badges.

**Decisions:** QA03 remains a contract/check slice, not a new UX surface. The
owner-run preflight prints a checklist derived from committed catalog metadata
because it cannot safely perform authenticated browser assertions without
operator credentials. It remains read-only and does not seed, duplicate, mutate
Auth0, mutate tenant data, or approve real-person data.

**Open questions raised:** None. Q-053 remains a real-person/legal-use gate
only and does not block synthetic/demo/sample/owner-controlled product work.

## 2026-05-16 - codex - SETUP04 post-duplicate setup action simplification

**Goal:** Make the editable setup route feel like the obvious next step after
`Duplicate as study` by making the current setup task lead inside preparation
actions.

**Did:**

- Added a Playwright structure contract in
  `apps/web/src/routes/product-surfaces.e2e.ts` for the selected setup route:
  `Study preparation` must appear before `Preparation actions`, and
  `Current setup task` must appear before `Setup path` and
  `Generated setup defaults`.
- Updated `apps/web/src/lib/product/SelectedSeriesSetupWorkflow.svelte` so
  setup managers see `Current setup task` first inside `Preparation actions`,
  then setup path/progress, then generated setup defaults as a secondary
  utility.
- Kept the read-only access message first for users without setup management
  access and kept setup path visible below it.

## 2026-05-17 - LS01 launch snapshot packet implementation draft

- Implemented `CampaignLaunchSnapshot.LaunchPacket` as a required JSON object with EF mapping and migration `20260517120000_AddCampaignLaunchSnapshotPacket`.

## 2026-05-17 - D145 assessment cadence and LS04 launch packet source provenance

Assessment: the new cadence was documented in `AGENTS.md` and the assessment archive README. Applying it immediately showed that launch packet source provenance is not just parity plumbing: it changes reporting/export semantics because migrated packets carry `provenance.source = migration_backfill` while runtime launch packets previously exposed only schema version and sections downstream.

Task: added D145 and implemented LS04. Launch-packet provenance responses now expose `source`; the shared projection parses `provenance.source`; runtime launch packets stamp `runtime_launch`; report-proof and response-export CSV/codebook contracts include `launch_packet_source`; product-surface launch-packet DTOs carry the same source.

## 2026-05-17 - D146 LS05 launch packet provenance safety sweep

The five tasks were sibling hardening items, so they stayed inline under D146.

Task 4: added the same explicit excluded-identifier contract for report-proof codebooks.

## 2026-05-17 - D147 invitation delivery runtime hardening

Assessment: after D146/LS05, the next non-UX risk was invitation delivery runtime safety. The C03/E01 foundation exists, but delivery attempts and proof responses are durable/operator-facing and sit next to raw respondent paths. V1 reference review was read-only and showed notification services quickly accumulate operational status/error surfaces, so v2 should sanitize delivery proof fields before SMTP use.

Task: added D147 and completed ten inline assessment/task pairs. Delivery processing now stores/returns stable sanitized errors, handles blank provider errors, redacts unsafe provider message IDs, bounds provider message IDs, exposes respondent paths only for local-dev proof, validates store-level batch size, requires live campaigns for delivery processing, allowlists provider names, validates SMTP from-address format, and validates SMTP port range.

Verification: RED `dotnet test --filter NotificationDeliveryContractTests` failed 11/11 expected checks before implementation. GREEN `dotnet test --filter NotificationDeliveryContractTests` passed 11/11. Existing `dotnet test --filter Email_delivery_processor` selected the Docker-backed processor test and skipped locally with 0 failures. `dotnet build` passed with 0 warnings/errors.

Remaining risk: Docker/Testcontainers-backed invitation delivery persistence coverage still needs a Docker-enabled run before treating the processor path as fully integration-validated.

Next: start D148 with a post-D147 notification/reminder runtime assessment. Prioritize Docker-backed delivery verification if the environment supports it, then assess reminder scheduling, unsubscribe/bounce/complaint boundaries, and delivery retry semantics before UX work resumes.

## 2026-05-17 - D148 notification/outbox runtime hardening

Assessment: after D147, the next non-UX runtime seam was asynchronous notification intent handling. `InvitationEmailQueued` outbox events, dispatcher diagnostics, retry/dead-letter storage, worker batch sizing, and SMTP configuration were adjacent to durable notification state and could become support/debug surfaces. Reminder scheduling, unsubscribe, bounce, complaint, and provider webhook handling remain future E01 work.

Task: added D148 and completed twenty inline assessment/task pairs. Outbox event creation now rejects blank/unsafe aggregate and event identifiers; durable retry and dead-letter errors use safe fallbacks, redaction, and control-character stripping; `InvitationEmailQueuedOutboxHandler` validates event type, schema version, notification id, and sensitive payload values before acknowledging; dispatcher registration and unknown-handler messages sanitize unsafe event names; worker options and direct relay calls cap batch size at 500; SMTP host values reject spaces/control characters; SMTP username/password must be configured together.

Verification: RED `dotnet test --filter "OutboxEventSafetyTests|InvitationEmailQueuedOutboxHandlerContractTests|OutboxRuntimeContractTests|EmailDeliveryOptionsContractTests"` failed before implementation with expected missing-contract failures. GREEN same filter passed UnitTests 10/10 and IntegrationTests 14/14. Related `dotnet test --filter "Outbox|NotificationDeliveryContractTests"` passed UnitTests 15/15 and IntegrationTests 34/34, with 7 Docker-backed Postgres tests skipped locally and 0 failures. `dotnet build` passed with 0 warnings/errors.

Remaining risk: Docker/Testcontainers-backed outbox relay, invitation batch, and delivery processor persistence tests still need a Docker-enabled run before treating the outbox/delivery persistence path as fully integration-validated.

Next: start D149 with a post-D148 notification/reminder runtime assessment. Prioritize Docker-backed outbox/delivery verification if available; otherwise assess reminder scheduling, delivery retry semantics, suppression/unsubscribe/bounce/complaint boundaries, and operational monitoring contracts.

## 2026-05-17 - D149 notification domain safety backstop

Assessment: after D147 store-level delivery hardening and D148 outbox/worker hardening, the remaining adjacent runtime risk was defense-in-depth inside the campaign domain entities. `Notification.MarkFailed` and `NotificationDeliveryAttempt` factories are durable write boundaries; future reminder/provider code could bypass the D147 store helper and persist raw provider errors, respondent paths, tokens, unsafe provider message ids, or unsafe provider names.

Task: added D149 and completed ten inline assessment/task pairs. Notification failure errors now use a stable fallback, redact sensitive values, and strip control characters. Failed delivery attempts do the same. Sent delivery attempts redact unsafe provider message ids, bound provider message ids, and both sent/failed delivery attempts sanitize provider names at the domain boundary.

Verification: RED `dotnet test --filter NotificationDeliveryDomainSafetyTests` first caught a test setup overload error, then failed 10/10 expected domain safety checks after correction. GREEN focused test passed 10/10. Related `dotnet test --filter "CampaignEntitiesTests|NotificationDeliveryContractTests|NotificationDeliveryDomainSafetyTests"` passed UnitTests 44/44 and IntegrationTests 11/11. `dotnet build` passed with 0 warnings/errors.

Remaining risk: Docker/Testcontainers-backed notification delivery persistence coverage still needs an enabled run before treating the end-to-end processor path as fully integration-validated. Reminder scheduling, unsubscribe, bounce, complaint, provider webhooks, and retry redesign remain future work.

Next: start D150 with a post-D149 notification/reminder runtime assessment. Prioritize Docker-backed outbox/delivery verification if available; otherwise assess reminder scheduling, retry semantics, suppression/unsubscribe/bounce/complaint boundaries, and operational monitoring contracts.

## 2026-05-17 - D150 product/runtime priority reassessment

Assessment: after D147-D149, the notification lane had reached diminishing returns for the current tenant-private proof spine. Invitation delivery is important because it touches tokens, respondent paths, and recipient metadata, but production reminders, bounce/complaint handling, unsubscribe/suppression, provider webhooks, and delivery monitoring are not the next highest-value agent-executable work.

Decision: added D150 and selected `SEC01 - Public respondent boundary abuse-control guardrails` as the next agent-executable slice. The active product vision remains tenant-private setup, frozen launch provenance, safe anonymous/anonymous-longitudinal collection, score materialization, disclosure-safe reports/waves/exports, and owner-run validation through ordinary product paths. Owner validation tasks O01/O02/O03/O05 remain the highest business priority but are owner-only. Generic designer/import UI, dashboards/PDFs, retention/withdrawal/legal semantics, deployment automation, and notification deliverability all remain important but are not selected now.

Next: run SEC01. Start with a public-boundary inventory and then harden missing abuse-control contracts for token validation, participant-code lookup, session creation, save, and submit. Keep it backend/runtime-focused, preserve neutral errors and log/diagnostic hygiene, avoid UI/UX changes, and run targeted tests/build plus Docker-backed coverage if the environment supports it.

True `anonymize` remains fail-closed because current response sessions still link through assignment, subject, consent, and participant-code relationships; silently deleting under an anonymize policy would be semantically wrong. ## 2026-05-17 - Codex

It does not add scheduler automation, legal workflow, anonymous withdrawal tokens, deployment, reports/PDFs, or UI/UX.

## 2026-05-17 - Codex

**Goal:** Implement RET09 retention due-candidate planning foundation without scheduler automation, tenant-facing legal/GDPR workflow, anonymous one-shot withdrawal tokens, deployment expansion, reports/PDF expansion, or UI/UX.

**Did:** Added RET09 design and implementation plans. Added `IRetentionDueCandidateStore`, safe due-candidate response contracts, and `RetentionDueCandidateStore`. The planner runs under tenant RLS, accepts explicit `as_of`, selects the latest usable campaign-series retention policy, supports `response_submitted_at` with `delete`/`anonymize`, computes due cutoffs from `retain_for_years`, and returns aggregate counts for consent records, response sessions, answers, score runs, scores, and platform-owned succeeded derived artifacts. Unsupported anchors/actions return safe diagnostics with zero counts. Added Docker-backed planner regression coverage to `WithdrawalRuntimeStoreTests` and registered the store in DI. Updated `NEXT-ACTIONS.md`, `NEXT-SESSION-PLAN.md`, current roadmap, and RET09 plan docs.

**Decisions:** RET09 is a read-only planner, not a scheduler or executor. It plans one campaign series at a time so future automation can enumerate series separately without hiding selection semantics. It returns policy and campaign-series identifiers plus aggregate entity counts only; it does not return subject, response-session, consent-record, answer, participant-code, token, recipient, provider, IP/user-agent, public-handle, or raw answer values. Q-053 still blocks real-person production data and external legal/GDPR claims.

**Verification:** RED Docker-backed due-candidate filter failed before implementation because planner types did not exist. GREEN elevated Docker-backed `RUN_POSTGRES_INTEGRATION_TESTS=1 dotnet test tests/Platform.IntegrationTests/Platform.IntegrationTests.csproj --no-restore --filter "FullyQualifiedName~Plan_due_candidates"` passed 5/5. ## 2026-05-17 - Codex

**Goal:** Finish the retention/withdrawal engineering workflow end-to-end through scheduler-safe due-batch automation, excluding Q-053 production legal approval and UI.

**Did:** Completed RET12, D164, RET13, D165, and RET14. RET12 added processing due-batch execution into delete/anonymize paths with parity recheck, derived-artifact invalidation, delivery identity scrubbing, safe completion/failure metadata, and migration `20260517195046_AddRetentionDueBatchExecutionMetadata`. D164 identified the repeated-anonymize scheduler risk. RET13 made anonymize due-candidate planning and due-batch execution graph selection exclude `response_session.anonymized_at IS NOT NULL` as already processed. D165 selected the automation boundary. RET14 added `RunDueBatchAutomationAsync`, a bounded caller-driven runner that scans tenant retention-policy series, plans due-batches idempotently, claims planned batches, executes processing batches, caps attempted work, and returns safe aggregate stage/result summaries. Added D164/D165 assessment docs and RET13/RET14 design/implementation plans. Updated schema, migration notes, roadmap, next actions, assessment index, and next-session handoff.

**Decisions:** Scheduler-safe automation should enter as a tested caller-driven runner, not an always-on hosted job. Already-anonymized response sessions are processed state for anonymize retention planning. Delete policies still select submitted rows that physically exist. Q-053 remains the gate for real-person production legal/GDPR claims; legal workflow, anonymous one-shot withdrawal tokens, deployment, reports/PDFs, and UI/UX remain unselected.

**Verification:** RET12 RED Docker-backed `Due_batch_execution` failed before implementation on missing method; GREEN `Due_batch_execution` passed 4/4; full `WithdrawalRuntimeStoreTests` passed 46/46. RET13 RED processed-state filter failed because anonymized sessions were still counted and duplicate due-batch planning succeeded; GREEN processed-state filter passed 2/2; full `WithdrawalRuntimeStoreTests` passed 48/48. RET14 RED `Due_batch_automation` failed before implementation on missing method; GREEN `Due_batch_automation` passed 2/2; final full Docker-backed `WithdrawalRuntimeStoreTests` passed 50/50. Final `dotnet build --no-restore` passed with 0 warnings and 0 errors.

**Next:** Run `D166 - Post-retention/withdrawal automation product/runtime assessment`. The retention/withdrawal engineering goal is complete from planner through automation runner; reassess the next non-UX limiter before selecting legal workflow, deployment, reports/PDFs, notification expansion, anonymous one-shot withdrawal tokens, or UI/UX.

## 2026-05-17 - Codex

**Goal:** Implement RET15 retention automation worker bootstrap without enabling destructive/anonymizing automation by default and without legal workflow, anonymous withdrawal tokens, notification expansion, deployment, reports/PDFs, or UI/UX.

**Did:** Added `IRetentionAutomationTenantSource`, an EF-backed `RetentionAutomationTenantSource`, disabled-by-default `RetentionAutomationWorkerOptions`, a `RetentionAutomationWorker` hosted service with testable `RunOnceAsync`, worker DI registration, worker host bootstrapping, and explicit disabled retention automation config in worker appsettings. Added RET15 unit tests under `tests/Platform.UnitTests/Workers/RetentionAutomationWorkerTests.cs`.

**Decisions:** RET15 uses the existing standard .NET hosted-service pattern rather than Hangfire/cron. It creates one scoped tick, enumerates tenant ids with usable retention policies or planned/processing due-batches, calls the RET14 runner with explicit caps, isolates tenant-level failures, and logs aggregate counts plus exception type/error code only. It deliberately avoids tenant ids in worker log messages and does not log raw exception messages.

**Verification:** RED `dotnet test tests/Platform.UnitTests/Platform.UnitTests.csproj --no-restore --filter "FullyQualifiedName~RetentionAutomationWorker"` failed before implementation on missing worker/source types. GREEN same filter passed 5/5. `dotnet build --no-restore` passed with 0 warnings and 0 errors.

**Next:** Run `D168 - Post-RET15 product/runtime assessment` before selecting legal/admin workflow, anonymous one-shot withdrawal tokens, notification/reminder expansion, deployment, reports/PDFs, UI/UX, or another runtime safety slice.

## 2026-05-18 - Codex

**Goal:** Implement RET16 tenant/admin withdrawal request intake foundation without anonymous public withdrawal tokens, notification expansion, reports/PDFs, deployment, or UI/UX.

**Did:** Added authenticated `POST /withdrawal-requests` under tenant context and `setup.manage`. Added `CreateTenantWithdrawalRequestCommand`, request/response contracts, and a store method for creating non-executing withdrawal request intent. Extended `withdrawal_event` with `requested` status, `response_session` target kind, typed `response_session_id`, migration `20260518090000_AddWithdrawalRequestIntake`, model snapshot updates, response-session tenant guardrails, safe actor/reason metadata, duplicate pending request idempotency, and aggregate-only response counts. Added Docker-backed runtime tests and API endpoint tests.

**Decisions:** RET16 records request intent only. `requested` events are intentionally outside existing planned/processing execution paths. Initial supported target is `response_session`; `consent_record` and public anonymous/self-service tokens remain later assessment candidates. Endpoint authorization reuses `setup.manage` because no narrower data-rights permission exists yet.

## 2026-05-18 - Codex

**Goal:** Implement RET18 withdrawal request decision foundation without execution mutation, anonymous public withdrawal tokens, notifications, reports/PDF/export, deployment, or UI/UX.

**Did:** Added requested-only approval and denial for reviewable `response_session` withdrawal requests. Domain now has explicit `denied` status plus `ApproveRequest` and `DenyRequest` transitions. EF model constraints and migration script coverage accept `denied` through migration `20260518103000_AddWithdrawalRequestDeniedStatus`. Store methods `ApproveWithdrawalRequestAsync` and `DenyWithdrawalRequestAsync` mutate only the request row status/metadata under tenant RLS, preserve wrong-tenant not-found behavior, and reject non-requested decisions. Added MediatR handlers and authenticated tenant/admin `POST /withdrawal-requests/{id}/approve` and `/withdrawal-requests/{id}/deny` endpoints under `setup.manage`. Updated `NEXT-ACTIONS.md`, `NEXT-SESSION-PLAN.md`, and `60-roadmap/current-roadmap.md`.

**Decisions:** RET18 is decision foundation only. Approval moves request intent to `planned` without adding response-session execution support; denial marks the request terminal with safe decision provenance. No execution mutation, scheduler behavior, anonymous public withdrawal tokens, notifications, reports/PDF/export, deployment, or UI/UX was added.

**Verification:** RED `WithdrawalEventLifecycle` failed on missing approve/deny/denied status. GREEN `WithdrawalEventLifecycle` passed 10/10. GREEN Docker-backed/API decision filter passed 15/15. Final `WithdrawalEventLifecycle` passed 10/10. ## 2026-05-18 - Codex

**Goal:** Implement RET19 response-session withdrawal execution support before anonymous tokens, notifications, reports/PDF/export, deployment, or UI/UX.

**Did:** Added Docker-backed response-session withdrawal execution tests for dry-run, claim, delete execution with artifact invalidation, anonymize execution with artifact invalidation, and graph-drift failure. Response-session anonymize keeps the response-session row and scrubs it through existing anonymization semantics.

## 2026-05-18 - Codex

**Goal:** Implement RET20 anonymous withdrawal request token foundation before notifications, reports/PDF/export, deployment, or UX.

**Did:** Added `WithdrawalRequestToken` domain entity, `WithdrawalRequestTokens` raw-token/hash helper, token issue/consume runtime contracts, public anonymous withdrawal request handler/endpoint, EF mapping, migration `20260518123000_AddWithdrawalRequestTokens`, model snapshot updates, runtime token issue/consume tests, endpoint tests, model/RLS assertions, and handoff updates.

**Decisions:** RET20 is public ingress only. It creates non-executing `requested` response-session withdrawal events through the existing tenant/admin lifecycle and consumes the one-shot token in the same transaction. It does not approve, deny, execute delete/anonymize, send notifications, add reports/PDF/export behavior, restart deployment automation, or add UX. V1 reference review found no useful withdrawal-token equivalent; v1 token examples store raw tokens and were not copied.

### EXP09 - Local private export artifact object store foundation

Goal: add a dependency-free private byte-store provider and wire external-object export artifact downloads through it.

Did: added `IExportArtifactObjectStore`, `ExportArtifactObjectStoreOptions`, and `LocalExportArtifactObjectStore` with strict object-key validation and safe missing-object errors. Registered the provider through Infrastructure DI under `ExportArtifacts:ObjectStore`. Updated `ReportProofExportStore` so inline artifacts still download from inline text, while external-object artifacts download through the provider only when one is available. Provider-backed external downloads verify byte size and SHA-256 checksum before returning `ContentBytes`; metadata `CanDownload` is provider-aware. Added object-store roundtrip/unsafe-key/missing-object tests and a Docker-backed external-object artifact download test.

## 2026-05-18 - EXP11 export artifact lifecycle attempt persistence foundation

Assessment/task pair: `D187` selected `EXP11` as the next reports/PDF/export runtime slice. The gap was durable failure-attempt state: after PDF04, renderer readiness was visible, but failed PDF generation/storage attempts still returned safe errors without creating auditable artifact lifecycle rows.

Implemented EXP11:
- `ReportProofExportStore` now validates the campaign-series reports workspace first, then persists safe failed `campaign_series_report_pdf` artifact rows for missing object-store dependency, missing renderer dependency, renderer failure, empty PDF bytes, and object-store write failure.
- Failed PDF artifact attempts use external-object storage kind with no storage key, no content, no checksum, no completed timestamp, zero byte/row counts, safe failure reason code, and aggregate-safe metadata only.
- Failed attempts remain non-downloadable and surface in the reports workspace with `CanDownload == false`.
- Relaxed the database storage-shape constraint so non-succeeded external-object artifact rows may omit storage keys; the domain still requires materialized storage/content for succeeded artifacts.

V1 read-only review: searched `../AnketasProject` report/PDF/export/support/GDPR/background-service surfaces. V1 has report/PDF models/services, support/GDPR request records, and background services, but no copyable durable failed-export artifact lifecycle. Lesson carried forward: v2 should preserve request/attempt auditability and safe failure metadata, while keeping raw renderer/store errors out of artifact metadata.

Verification:
- RED: `RUN_POSTGRES_INTEGRATION_TESTS=1 dotnet test tests/Platform.IntegrationTests/Platform.IntegrationTests.csproj --filter "FullyQualifiedName~Campaign_series_report_pdf_artifact"` failed 2/7 as expected before implementation.
- GREEN: same PDF artifact lifecycle filter passed 7/7.
- GREEN: `Campaign_series_reports_workspace_excludes_sensitive_fields` passed 1/1.
- GREEN: `RlsMigrationScriptTests` passed 21/21.
- GREEN: `ExportArtifactTests` passed 15/15.
- GREEN: `dotnet build --no-restore` passed with 0 warnings and 0 errors.

Next: start `D188 - Post-EXP11 product/runtime assessment`. Reassess before selecting async export workers, S3-compatible storage, signed URLs, notifications, deployment/operability, withdrawal-trust expansion, or support UX.

## 2026-05-18 - D188 post-EXP11 product/runtime assessment

Assessment/task pair: after EXP11, the next gap is that campaign-series report PDF lifecycle is still request-bound. Failed and successful PDF artifacts are durable, but `queued` and `running` states are not yet used by a worker boundary.

Decision: select `EXP12 - Campaign-series report PDF queued worker foundation`.

Why this next:
- S3-compatible storage and signed URLs improve infrastructure, but do not fix synchronous generation.
- Notifications need a durable completion/failure boundary to notify from.
- UX should follow runtime semantics rather than invent frontend state around synchronous proof actions.
- Deployment automation remains owner-deferred for DEP01-C.
- Withdrawal trust remains important, but RET16-RET19 moved that lane substantially; the fresh blocker is reports/PDF artifact lifecycle depth.

## 2026-05-18 - D191 post-EXP14 product/runtime assessment

## 2026-05-18 - EXP15 report PDF artifact stale-rendering recovery guard

Assessment/task pair: `D191` selected `EXP15` because the PDF artifact worker could leave rows stuck in `rendering` if interrupted after claim and before terminal success/failure.

Implemented EXP15:
- Added `ReportPdfArtifacts:RenderingTimeoutMinutes` with positive option validation and default config.
- Tenant discovery now includes queued PDF artifacts and stale rendering PDF artifacts older than the timeout cutoff.
- Worker ticks pass a stale-rendering cutoff, fail stale rendering artifacts first, count recovered artifacts, then continue queued processing.
- Store recovery marks stale rendering rows failed with safe reason `report_pdf.rendering_timeout`, clears materialized content/storage fields, and leaves recent rendering rows untouched.

## 2026-05-18 - D194 post-EXP17 product/runtime assessment

Assessment/task pair: after EXP17, failed PDF artifacts have a manual retry queue action, but the outbox runtime can still be unhealthy without readiness reflecting that stuck state.

Decision: select `OPS05 - Outbox dead-letter readiness guard`.

Rationale:
- Report/PDF terminal intents and future notification/admin flows depend on outbox reliability.
- Pending unpublished outbox rows can be normal during load.
- Dead-lettered unpublished rows represent exhausted retry/stuck async work and should block readiness before broader worker heartbeat/backlog metrics, real notification delivery/routing, S3-compatible storage, signed URLs, withdrawal expansion, or UX.

Added assessment `docs/v2/80-agent-handoff/assessments/D194-POST-EXP17-PRODUCT-RUNTIME-ASSESSMENT-2026-05-18.md` and plan `docs/plans/2026-05-18-ops05-outbox-dead-letter-readiness-guard.md`.

Next: implement OPS05 test-first.

## 2026-05-18 - OPS05 outbox dead-letter readiness guard

Assessment/task pair: `D194` selected `OPS05` because outbox dead letters are now a cross-lane runtime risk for report/PDF, notification, retention, withdrawal, and future admin workflows.

Implemented OPS05:
- Added `OutboxDeadLetterHealthCheck`.
- Registered `outbox_dead_letters` through existing `IPlatformHealthCheck` readiness/startup composition.
- The check fails closed on query errors, reports `unready` for unpublished dead-lettered outbox events, and keeps ordinary unpublished/pending events ready.
- Health responses remain name/status only and do not expose tenant ids, event types, payloads, counts, last errors, SQL details, raw tokens, participant codes, recipients, or provider diagnostics.

## 2026-05-18 - D195 post-OPS05 product/runtime assessment

Assessment/task pair: after OPS05, dead-lettered outbox work blocks readiness, but the outbox runtime signal is still embedded in a single health check instead of being reusable for metrics, runbook support, admin views, or later worker heartbeat/readiness policy.

Decision: select `OPS06 - Outbox operational snapshot foundation`.

Rationale:
- Worker heartbeat needs a separate durable identity and stale-threshold policy.
- Real notification routing still needs recipient and notification-table semantics.
- S3-compatible storage and signed URLs do not improve operator visibility into the async spine.
- A reusable aggregate-only outbox snapshot is the safer next primitive.

Next: implement OPS06 test-first.

## 2026-05-18 - D196 post-OPS06 product/runtime assessment

Assessment/task pair: after OPS06, aggregate outbox runtime state is reusable, but readiness still only catches dead-letter exhaustion. Due outbox work can stop draining before dead letters appear.

Decision: select `OPS07 - Outbox due-backlog readiness guard`.

Rationale:
- Oldest due outbox age is a bounded leading indicator from the OPS06 snapshot.
- A worker heartbeat table still needs durable worker identity, expected-worker configuration, and stale-threshold semantics.
- Health output should remain name/status only, not expose counts or timestamps.

Added assessment `docs/v2/80-agent-handoff/assessments/D196-POST-OPS06-PRODUCT-RUNTIME-ASSESSMENT-2026-05-18.md` and plan `docs/plans/2026-05-18-ops07-outbox-due-backlog-readiness-guard.md`.

Next: implement OPS07 test-first.

## 2026-05-18 - OPS07 outbox due-backlog readiness guard

Assessment/task pair: `D196` selected `OPS07` because due backlog age is now available through OPS06 and can identify worker-drain failure before dead-letter exhaustion.

Implemented OPS07:
- Added `OutboxOperationalReadinessOptions`.
- Added `OutboxDueBacklogHealthCheck` named `outbox_due_backlog`.
- Registered options validation and health check in Infrastructure DI.
- The check returns `ok` for no due backlog or recent due backlog, `unready` for stale due backlog, and fails closed if snapshot retrieval fails.

## 2026-05-18 - D198 post-QA12 product/runtime assessment

Assessment/task pair: after QA12, the outbox health surface was safe, but verification showed readiness latency risk because two outbox health checks could independently query the same aggregate snapshot.

Decision: select `OPS08 - Outbox operational snapshot per-request query cache`.

Rationale:
- Separate `outbox_dead_letters` and `outbox_due_backlog` checks remain useful for diagnosis.
- They should share one scoped aggregate snapshot inside a request.
- This makes the existing readiness design efficient before adding worker heartbeat/readiness policy or metrics/admin surfaces.

Added assessment `docs/v2/80-agent-handoff/assessments/D198-POST-QA12-PRODUCT-RUNTIME-ASSESSMENT-2026-05-18.md` and plan `docs/plans/2026-05-18-ops08-outbox-operational-snapshot-per-request-query-cache.md`.

Next: implement OPS08 test-first.

## 2026-05-18 - OPS08 outbox operational snapshot per-request query cache

Assessment/task pair: `D198` selected `OPS08` because duplicated snapshot DB work was the next concrete operability risk after QA12.

Implemented OPS08:
- Added per-instance memoization to scoped `OutboxOperationalSnapshotStore`.
- `outbox_dead_letters` and `outbox_due_backlog` now share one snapshot task/result inside a health request scope.
- Snapshot query failures remain fail-closed because both health checks observe the same failed task.

## 2026-05-18 - D199 post-OPS08 product/runtime assessment

Assessment/task pair: after OPS08, outbox readiness checks are efficient, but due-backlog readiness still cannot prove the worker process is alive when queues are empty.

Decision: select `OPS09 - Worker process heartbeat writer foundation`.

Rationale:
- API stale-heartbeat readiness needs deployment-topology policy first.
- The bounded prerequisite is a durable heartbeat signal written by the worker process.
- Heartbeat identity must avoid hostnames, machine paths, tenant ids, payloads, or secrets.

Added assessment `docs/v2/80-agent-handoff/assessments/D199-POST-OPS08-PRODUCT-RUNTIME-ASSESSMENT-2026-05-18.md` and plan `docs/plans/2026-05-18-ops09-worker-process-heartbeat-writer-foundation.md`.

Next: implement OPS09 test-first.

## 2026-05-18 - D200 post-OPS09 product/runtime assessment

Assessment/task pair: after OPS09, worker heartbeat rows are durable, but API readiness still cannot enforce the signal when deployment topology expects the worker host.

Decision: select `OPS10 - Worker heartbeat readiness guard foundation`.

Rationale:
- Heartbeat readiness must be config-gated because local/dev and proof environments may not always run the worker host.
- The check should be disabled by default and enabled only when the worker host is required.
- Public health output must stay name/status only.

Added assessment `docs/v2/80-agent-handoff/assessments/D200-POST-OPS09-PRODUCT-RUNTIME-ASSESSMENT-2026-05-18.md` and plan `docs/plans/2026-05-18-ops10-worker-heartbeat-readiness-guard-foundation.md`.

## 2026-05-18 - D202 post-QA13 product/runtime assessment

Assessment/task pair: after QA13, deployment/operability was coherent enough to pivot back to report/export delivery. The next limiter was private external-object storage still being local-filesystem only.

Decision: select `EXP18 - S3-compatible export artifact object-store provider foundation`.

Rationale:
- PDF artifacts are durable, observable, worker-ready, and retriable.
- Signed URLs and production deployment need a non-local object-store provider first.
- Real notification routing can announce artifact completion later, but successful delivery storage is the narrower prerequisite.

## 2026-05-18 - D321/D322 campaign failed-delivery requeue no-op evidence

Assessment/task pair: D321 selected D322 because successful local-dev campaign email delivery was already explicit in product-spine evidence, but the failed-delivery requeue endpoint was not represented in owner-runnable release evidence. Actual SMTP delivery, real failed-delivery recovery, remote proof, Q-053 legal/GDPR claims, and Q-054 outbound operational-notification email remained blocked or unproven.

Implemented D322:
- Extended `deploy/staging/smoke-product-spine.ps1` so `Assert-CampaignEmailInvitationDelivery` calls `POST /campaigns/{id}/notification-deliveries/requeue-failed` after clean local-dev delivery.
- Required a safe no-op response with `requestedBatchSize = 5` and `requeuedCount = 0`.
- Added safe-output checks so the requeue response does not expose invitation ids, SMTP values, passwords/secrets, storage keys, connection strings, or tenant ids.
- Added `failedRequeueNoopProven` and `failedRequeueNoopRequeuedCount` to `campaignEmailDeliveryProof`.
- Extended `deploy/staging/verify-release-evidence.ps1` to require the no-op proof fields while preserving `failedRequeueRecoveryProven = false`.

## 2026-05-18 - D203 post-EXP18 product/runtime assessment

Assessment/task pair: after EXP18, private S3-compatible object storage exists, but report/export delivery still lacks a short-lived artifact access primitive for future notifications, admin delivery flows, and deployment runbooks.

Decision: select `EXP19 - Export artifact signed download URL foundation`.

Rationale:
- Signed URLs are the bounded next report/export delivery primitive after private object storage.
- Real notification routing should not be asked to carry raw storage keys or depend on public buckets.
- An authenticated API endpoint can come later; first the provider-level capability needs to exist and be tested.

## 2026-05-18 - D204 post-EXP19 product/runtime assessment

Assessment/task pair: after EXP19, S3-compatible signed URL generation existed at the provider layer, but no authenticated API route could issue a short-lived artifact URL after tenant, permission, and artifact-state checks.

Decision: select `EXP20 - Authenticated export artifact signed download URL endpoint`.

## 2026-05-18 - D205 post-EXP20 product/runtime assessment

Assessment/task pair: after EXP20, the platform could issue signed URLs through an authenticated endpoint, but signed URLs expose the object path and the current PDF object-key shape included tenant and campaign-series GUIDs.

Decision: select `EXP21 - Signed download storage-key privacy hardening`.

Rationale:
- The key shape was safe enough for server-side private object reads but too context-rich for capability URLs.
- Notification routing should not spread tenant/campaign-series identifiers through signed-link paths.
- The provider should remain storage-key agnostic; eligibility belongs in the report/export store.

## 2026-05-18 - D206 post-EXP21 product/runtime assessment

Assessment/task pair: after EXP21, signed download links were safer, but the next notification step needed a clean destination. The existing `notification` table is invitation-email specific and requires campaign, assignment, and recipient fields.

Decision: select `NOTIF02 - Report PDF terminal operational notification foundation`.

Rationale:
- Report/PDF terminal events are product/runtime notifications, not respondent invitation deliveries.
- Reusing the existing `notification` table would conflate recipient email delivery with internal operational events.
- A separate durable operational notification table creates the right foundation before APIs, email routing, or UX.

Added assessment `docs/v2/80-agent-handoff/assessments/D206-POST-EXP21-PRODUCT-RUNTIME-ASSESSMENT-2026-05-18.md` and plan `docs/plans/2026-05-18-notif02-report-pdf-terminal-operational-notification-foundation.md`.

## 2026-05-18 - D207 post-NOTIF02 product/runtime assessment

Assessment/task pair: after NOTIF02, operational notifications were durable but invisible to authenticated product/runtime consumers. Email routing and UX were premature without a safe backend read contract.

Decision: select `NOTIF03 - Operational notification read API foundation`.

Rationale:
- A manager read API is the smallest useful consumer boundary over the durable notification table.
- It lets future UX or runbooks use operational notifications without depending on outbox/storage internals.
- Email routing still needs recipient policy, copy, expiry expectations, and escalation decisions.

## 2026-05-18 - D211 post-NOTIF05 product/runtime assessment

Assessment/task pair: after NOTIF05, operational notifications had durable routing, read API, mark-read, leak hardening, and unread summary. Anonymous withdrawal tokens were not actually missing; RET20 and QA09 already added and safety-swept that lane. Continuing notification features by inertia would have been lower value than checking the connected runtime seams.

Decision: select `QA15 - Cross-lane product/runtime regression sweep`.

Rationale:
- Withdrawal trust is coherent enough for now after RET16-RET20 plus QA07-QA09.
- Reports/PDF/export value advanced through governed artifacts, workers, object storage, signed URLs, and storage-key privacy.
- Notifications now have enough backend foundation for operators/runbooks without email or UX.
- Deployment automation remains owner-deferred.
- A cross-lane sweep gives better control before selecting the next feature lane.

Added assessment `docs/v2/80-agent-handoff/assessments/D211-POST-NOTIF05-PRODUCT-RUNTIME-ASSESSMENT-2026-05-18.md` and plan `docs/plans/2026-05-18-qa15-cross-lane-product-runtime-regression-sweep.md`.

## 2026-05-18 - D212 post-QA15 product/runtime assessment

Assessment/task pair: after QA15 passed, the next limiter was not another notification feature. The worker host had become product-critical because it owns outbox relay, worker heartbeat, retention automation, and report PDF artifact processing, but the staging deployment package still had no worker Dockerfile or Compose service.

Decision: select `OPS11 - Staging worker deployment foundation`.

Rationale:
- Existing async/runtime capabilities were verified but not fully deployable through staging Compose.
- Worker deployment is a real operability gap and does not reopen owner-deferred GitHub Actions or registry promotion.
- Notification filters, email routing, report UX, and broad UI/UX are less important while the worker host is missing from staging.

Added assessment `docs/v2/80-agent-handoff/assessments/D212-POST-QA15-PRODUCT-RUNTIME-ASSESSMENT-2026-05-18.md` and plan `docs/plans/2026-05-18-ops11-staging-worker-deployment-foundation.md`.

Next: implement OPS11 test-first.

- Added `deploy/staging/worker.Dockerfile`.

- Enabled outbox relay and worker heartbeat by default.
- Kept retention automation and report PDF artifact processing disabled unless explicitly enabled.

- Added local/VPS env example worker defaults.
- Updated the VPS staging runbook.
- Added static deployment package tests.

- GREEN focused deployment package tests passed 4/4.

## 2026-05-18 - D213 post-OPS11 product/runtime assessment

Assessment/task pair: after OPS11, staging could build and run the worker host with safe defaults, but the local staging smoke still verified only API/web behavior. It would not fail if the worker service was missing, exited, or not running.

Decision: select `OPS12 - Staging worker smoke coverage`.

Rationale:

- A cheap local smoke assertion catches missing/exited worker containers before deeper product checks.
- This stays within operability and does not reopen GitHub Actions, registry promotion, live VPS deployment, notification email routing, or UX.

## 2026-05-18 - OPS12 staging worker smoke coverage

Assessment/task pair: `D213` selected OPS12 because local staging smoke needed to prove the new worker service is actually running.

Implemented OPS12:
- Extended `deploy/staging/smoke-local-staging.ps1` with `Assert-ComposeServiceRunning`.
- The smoke now runs `docker compose --env-file $envFile -f $composeFile ps --status running --services`.
- The smoke fails unless `worker` is present in the running Compose services before API/web assertions.
- No retention automation, report PDF artifact processing, GitHub Actions, live VPS provisioning, notification email routing, report/export features, withdrawal workflow changes, or UX was added.

## 2026-05-18 - D214 post-OPS12 product/runtime assessment

Assessment/task pair: after OPS12, local staging smoke checked that the worker service was running, but it still did not prove the worker host could load configuration, connect to Postgres, and write the durable heartbeat introduced by OPS09/OPS10.

Decision: select `OPS13 - Local staging worker heartbeat smoke`.

Rationale:
- Worker container presence is weaker evidence than a durable heartbeat row.
- Heartbeat writing is safe and non-destructive.
- This keeps deployment/operability work bounded and avoids enabling retention automation or report PDF artifact processing by default.

Added assessment `docs/v2/80-agent-handoff/assessments/D214-POST-OPS12-PRODUCT-RUNTIME-ASSESSMENT-2026-05-18.md` and plan `docs/plans/2026-05-18-ops13-local-staging-worker-heartbeat-smoke.md`.

Next: implement OPS13 test-first.

## 2026-05-18 - OPS13 local staging worker heartbeat smoke

Assessment/task pair: `D214` selected OPS13 because local staging smoke needed durable worker heartbeat evidence, not just container state.

Implemented OPS13:
- Added `Wait-WorkerHeartbeat` to `deploy/staging/smoke-local-staging.ps1`.
- The smoke now queries local Compose Postgres for `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM worker_heartbeat WHERE worker_name = :'worker_name';`.
- The smoke waits for the configured `WorkerHeartbeat__WorkerName` and fails if no heartbeat row appears.
- The query uses count-only evidence and does not print secrets or row internals.
- No retention automation, report PDF artifact processing, API readiness enforcement, GitHub Actions, live VPS deployment, notification email routing, report/export feature, withdrawal workflow change, or UX was added.

Verification:
- RED focused deployment package tests failed on the missing heartbeat smoke assertion.
- GREEN focused deployment package tests passed 6/6.
- `dotnet build --no-restore` passed with 0 warnings and 0 errors.

## 2026-05-18 - D217 post-NOTIF06 product/runtime assessment

Assessment/task pair: after NOTIF06, product-spine smoke proved that report PDF terminal events reached operational notifications. It still did not prove the artifact delivery/failure boundary: a succeeded PDF should download as PDF, while a failed PDF should expose safe failure metadata.

Decision: select `PDF06 - Product-spine report PDF delivery/failure smoke`.

Rationale:
- Direct authenticated artifact download is the stable local/staging delivery contract.
- Signed URLs are intentionally unsupported under local/default object storage, so signed URL smoke is not the next best local product-spine check.
- Accepting either succeeded-download or failed-with-safe-code preserves robust smoke behavior across renderer/storage topologies.

Added assessment `docs/v2/80-agent-handoff/assessments/D217-POST-NOTIF06-PRODUCT-RUNTIME-ASSESSMENT-2026-05-18.md` and plan `docs/plans/2026-05-18-pdf06-product-spine-report-pdf-delivery-failure-smoke.md`.

- Added `Assert-ReportPdfArtifactDeliveryState` to `deploy/staging/smoke-product-spine.ps1`.
- Succeeded PDF artifacts are downloaded through `/export-artifacts/{id}/download`.
- The download response must be HTTP 200 with `application/pdf`.
- Failed PDF artifacts must include a non-empty safe `failureReasonCode`.
- No signed URLs, public links, email routing, enabled PDF worker automation, deployment automation, or UX was added.

## 2026-05-18 - D219 post-QA16 product/runtime assessment

Assessment/task pair: after QA16, the edited smoke scripts parsed and the build was green. The next report/export deployment gap was local object-store sharing across the now-separate API and worker containers.

Decision: select `OPS14 - Shared local export artifact object-store volume`.

Rationale:
- Local object storage is filesystem-backed.
- In Compose, API and worker have separate filesystems unless a shared volume is mounted.
- Worker-created external-object artifacts must remain downloadable by the API.
- This is a prerequisite before intentionally enabling report PDF worker automation or deeper signed URL smoke.

Added assessment `docs/v2/80-agent-handoff/assessments/D219-POST-QA16-PRODUCT-RUNTIME-ASSESSMENT-2026-05-18.md` and plan `docs/plans/2026-05-18-ops14-shared-local-export-artifact-object-store-volume.md`.

## 2026-05-18 - D228 report/export live staging worker operability

**Goal:** Prove the connected local staging product spine after static deployment gates were green: API, worker, Postgres, outbox relay, operational notifications, report/export artifacts, and smoke scripts.

**Did:** Created `docs/v2/80-agent-handoff/assessments/D228-REPORT-EXPORT-LIVE-STAGING-WORKER-OPERABILITY-ASSESSMENT-2026-05-18.md` and `docs/plans/2026-05-18-d228-report-export-live-staging-worker-operability.md`. Updated `docs/v2/80-agent-handoff/NEXT-ACTIONS.md` and `docs/v2/80-agent-handoff/NEXT-SESSION-PLAN.md`. Implemented the D228 runtime fixes across staging Compose/env/role scripts, outbox relay tenant processing, worker-role RLS policy, heartbeat audit exclusion, operational notification transaction reuse, and local/product-spine smoke scripts.

**Decisions:** Keep API runtime role least-privilege. Use a dedicated `platform_worker` role for worker operational duties. Add a narrow due-outbox worker relay policy without `BYPASSRLS`. Process one tenant per relay scope/tick with application and database tenant context established. Keep `WorkerHeartbeat` tenantless and non-audited. Let operational notification recording reuse an existing tenant transaction when called from the worker relay.

**Open questions raised:** None.

**Verification:** Focused staging worker deployment tests passed 18/18. Focused RLS/outbox runtime tests passed. Docker-backed outbox relay, report-PDF terminal notification, heartbeat, and operational notification transaction tests passed. Runtime outbox/report-PDF handler contract tests passed 38/38. Deployment-package Node tests passed 24/24. Local and VPS Compose config rendered. Local staging smoke passed. Product-spine smoke passed. `dotnet build --no-restore` passed with 0 warnings and 0 errors.

**Next:** Start `D229 - Withdrawal trust closure assessment after live staging proof`. Reassess authenticated tenant/admin withdrawal request lifecycle, anonymous withdrawal-token residuals, audit/anonymization safety, retention integration, notification needs, and remaining non-UX closure gaps before returning to report/export residuals, deployment/operability, notifications, or UX.

## 2026-05-18 - D230 authenticated withdrawal request token issue API foundation

## 2026-05-18 - D232 product-spine withdrawal token issue/consume smoke coverage

**Goal:** Prove authenticated withdrawal token issue plus public anonymous consume through the live local staging product-spine smoke before building UI/email delivery.

**Did:** Extended `deploy/staging/smoke-product-spine.ps1` with `Assert-WithdrawalTokenIssueAndConsume` and called it after submitted responses exist. Added static coverage in `tests/Platform.UnitTests/Deployment/StagingWorkerDeploymentPackageTests.cs`. Live smoke exposed a backend bug in anonymous consume audit context. Added Docker-backed regression `Anonymous_withdrawal_token_consume_sets_application_tenant_context_for_audit` in `tests/Platform.IntegrationTests/Infrastructure/WithdrawalRuntimeStoreTests.cs`. Fixed `src/Platform.Infrastructure/Retention/WithdrawalRuntimeStore.cs` to accept optional `ICurrentTenant`, set application tenant context from the parsed withdrawal token tenant during anonymous consume, and fail closed if an existing application tenant context does not match the token tenant.

**Decisions:** Keep D232 as backend/live-smoke proof only. No respondent UI, email delivery, token listing/recovery, automatic token issuance, approval, or delete/anonymize execution was added. The token-derived tenant is the correct application tenant context for anonymous consume because the raw token is already tenant-hinted, hash-validated, unexpired, unconsumed, and tied to a tenant-scoped response session under RLS.

**Open questions raised:** None.

**Verification:** RED focused static test failed on missing `Assert-WithdrawalTokenIssueAndConsume`. GREEN focused static test passed. Full `StagingWorkerDeploymentPackageTests` passed 19/19. RED Docker-backed audit regression failed on missing store constructor/context path. GREEN audit regression passed 1/1. Docker-backed `Anonymous_withdrawal_token` filter passed 8/8. Staging API/worker rebuild passed. Live `powershell -NoProfile -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File deploy\staging\smoke-product-spine.ps1` passed. `dotnet build --no-restore` passed with 0 warnings and 0 errors.

## 2026-05-18 - D233 post-D232 product/runtime assessment

**Goal:** Reassess product/runtime priority after D232 proved live withdrawal token issue/consume.

**Did:** Created `docs/v2/80-agent-handoff/assessments/D233-POST-D232-PRODUCT-RUNTIME-ASSESSMENT-2026-05-18.md` and `docs/plans/2026-05-18-d234-withdrawal-request-execution-api-foundation.md`. Updated `docs/v2/80-agent-handoff/NEXT-ACTIONS.md` and `docs/v2/80-agent-handoff/NEXT-SESSION-PLAN.md`.

**Decisions:** Selected `D234 - Tenant/admin withdrawal request execution API foundation`. The runtime store has safe response-session execution primitives and Docker-backed tests, but the tenant/admin API exposes only create/list/get/approve/deny, token issue, and anonymous consume. ## 2026-05-18 - D238 withdrawal terminal operational notifications

**Goal:** Add durable in-app operational notification evidence when withdrawal requests reach terminal denied, completed, or failed states.

Updated `docs/v2/80-agent-handoff/NEXT-ACTIONS.md` and `docs/v2/80-agent-handoff/NEXT-SESSION-PLAN.md`.

Payloads stay pointer-only: schema version, withdrawal request id, target kind, requested action, and status. ## 2026-05-18 - D254 product-spine withdrawal derived artifact invalidation smoke

Assessment/task pair: `D253` selected `D254` because Docker-backed withdrawal runtime tests proved derived artifact invalidation, but the owner-runnable product-spine smoke did not prove pre-withdrawal report/response export artifacts become deleted/non-downloadable after withdrawal execution.

Implemented D254:
- Added `Assert-WithdrawalInvalidatedDerivedArtifacts` to `deploy/staging/smoke-product-spine.ps1`.
- After withdrawal execution, the smoke refetches the affected wave-1 report export, pre-withdrawal campaign-series response export, and post-close campaign-series response export artifacts.
- The helper asserts deleted status, `canDownload=false`, `deletedAt`, cleared checksum, empty CSV payload, and no value/secret leakage.
- Added static deployment-package coverage in `StagingWorkerDeploymentPackageTests`.

Debugging note:
- Initial live smoke failed because the helper asserted against retained codebook metadata. Database/API evidence showed the existing invalidation contract clears content, checksum, and downloadability while retaining codebook metadata. The helper was narrowed to that established contract.
- A second live run showed `csvContent` is returned as an empty string, not omitted; the helper now treats null/empty/whitespace as cleared.
- A third live run showed response-export codebook metadata can contain the field name `recipient`; the sensitive check now looks for value/secret markers rather than expected metadata field names.

Verification:
- RED focused static deployment test failed on missing invalidation smoke.
- GREEN focused static deployment test passed.
- Full `StagingWorkerDeploymentPackageTests` passed 25/25.
- PowerShell parser validation passed.
- Live `smoke-product-spine.ps1` passed and proved withdrawal-derived artifact invalidation through local staging.
- `dotnet build --no-restore` passed with 0 warnings and 0 errors.

Remaining risk: No report PDF invalidation change, new withdrawal mutation semantics, UI/UX, notification email routing, or legal/GDPR production claim was added. Next queue head is `D255 - Post-D254 product/runtime assessment`.

## 2026-05-18 - D256 product-spine withdrawal report PDF artifact invalidation smoke

## 2026-05-18 - D305 post-D304 product/runtime assessment

Assessment: D304 made local/default release evidence, product-spine evidence, and backup/restore evidence structured and self-verifying. The remaining unblocked deployment/operability evidence gap is target-environment remote preflight detail: when owner-supplied origins are available, the release runner can execute remote preflight, but the safe check details remain console-only.

## 2026-05-18 - D306 remote preflight structured evidence sidecar

- Added optional `-EvidencePath` to `deploy/staging/smoke-validation-demo-preflight.ps1`.

- Extended `deploy/staging/verify-release-evidence.ps1 -RequireRemotePreflight` to require and validate the sidecar when remote preflight is expected.
- Updated `docs/v2/40-ops/vps-staging-runbook.md` and narrowed the Node deployment-package read-only guard so only the dedicated evidence writer may write.

- Full default `run-release-checks.ps1 -EvidencePath <temp>` passed with 14 gates, 1 remote-preflight skip, product-spine sidecar validated, backup/restore sidecar validated, and remote sidecar not required because no remote origins were supplied.
